content.text:
<think>
Examining the original chart carefully, I can see stacked area charts for each country showing the percentage composition of beer (orange), spirits (red), wine (light blue), and other (remaining colors) from 1965-2015.

Looking across all countries in the chart:
- Australia, Austria, Belgium, Canada, Czechia all show relatively stable spirits consumption
- Brazil shows some decline in spirits but not dramatic
- Chile shows a moderate decline in spirits
- Costa Rica shows a noticeable decline in spirits
- Cuba and Czechia have minimal spirits throughout
- **China shows an exceptionally dramatic change**: The chart is dominated by a large red (spirits) section in the early years (1965-1970s), representing the majority of alcohol consumption. Then, over a very short period (by approximately 1975-1980), the red section almost completely disappears. The composition shifts dramatically from spirits-dominant to wine-dominant (blue) and beer-dominant (orange).

China's transformation is the most striking - the red spirits area goes from occupying roughly 50%+ of the chart to nearly 0% in less than 15 years.
</think>

<answer>
China shows the most dramatic drop in the percentage of spirits consumption. In the early 1970s, spirits (red) dominated China's alcohol consumption, representing a large portion of the chart. By the early 1980s, spirits consumption had nearly completely disappeared, with the chart becoming dominated by wine (blue) and beer (orange) instead. This transformation occurred over approximately 10-15 years, making it the most striking change visible in the entire dataset.
</answer>
